solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2224D - Zhily and Barknights
Fix $$$i  \lt  j$$$. After randomly permuting $$$b$$$, each ordered pair $$$(b_k, b_l)$$$ with $$$k \ne l$$$ is assigned to positions $$$(i, j)$$$ with probability $$$\frac{1}{n (n - 1)}$$$.
Therefore, the expected number of inversions equals
$$$$$$ \frac{1}{n (n - 1)}\sum_{i  \lt  j, k \ne l}[a_i b_k  \gt  a_j b_l]. $$$$$$
The square brackets in $$$[a_i b_k  \gt  a_j b_l]$$$ denote the Iverson bracket, an indicator function that evaluates to $$$1$$$ if the inequality is true and $$$0$$$ otherwise.
Solution 1
:
Rewrite the inequality $$$a_i b_k  \gt  a_j b_l$$$ as $$$\frac{a_i}{a_j}  \gt  \frac{b_l}{b_k}.$$$
Compute all values $$$\frac{a_i}{a_j}$$$ for $$$i  \lt  j$$$, and all values $$$\frac{b_l}{b_k}$$$ for $$$k \ne l$$$. Sort both of these resulting sequences and use a two-pointer technique to count the number of valid pairs.
The time complexity depends on the sorting method. For example, using
std::sort
, we can achieve an $$$O(n^2\log n)$$$ approach, which can solve the problem within $$$0.6\text{s}$$$ and $$$50\text{MB}$$$.
Solution 2
:
Consider all pairs $$$(i, k)$$$ and form a list of 2D elements $$$(a_i b_k, i)$$$.
Counting the number of quadruples $$$(i, j, k, l)$$$ with $$$i  \lt  j$$$ and $$$a_i b_k  \gt  a_j b_l$$$ is equivalent to counting dominating pairs among these elements. However, we must subtract the cases where $$$k = l$$$. The number of such cases is equal to $$$n$$$ times the inversion count of sequence $$$a$$$.
Dominating pairs can be counted using a Fenwick tree or merge sort in $$$O(n^2\log n)$$$ time, and this approach takes only $$$0.4\text{s}$$$.
